context: turn to the book of Philippians and then we'll make one last reference to Psalm 25 before we have our prayer time this evening. But look, if you will, in Philippians chapter number four. Philippians four is a great text of scripture dealing with the subject matter of peace.
point_summary: Peace is not found in the absence of trouble but in the person of Jesus Christ, a truth Paul's prison-written words in Philippians 4 are introduced to confirm.

context: rejoice in the Lord always. And again, I say, rejoice

context: rejoice in the Lord always. And again, I say rejoice

context: be careful for nothing. Literally worry about nothing

context: the peace of God which passeth all understanding, it's going to keep your heart and mind through Christ Jesus

context: Truly brethren, whatsoever things are true, whatsoever things are honest, whatsoever things are just, whatsoever things are pure, whatsoever things are lovely, whatsoever things are of good report, if there be any virtue and if there be any praise, think on these things

context: the peace of God which passeth all understanding

context: Now ye Philippians know also, that in the beginning of the gospel, when I departed from Macedonia, no church communicated with me as concerning giving and receiving, but ye only.
point_summary: Philippians 4:15 records Paul's striking acknowledgment that, at a specific moment early in his gospel ministry, the Philippian church alone partnered with him financially when no other church did.

context: Paul writes, now ye Philippians know also that in the beginning of the Gospel when I departed from Macedonia, no church, watch this next word now, don't miss it, communicated
point_summary: The Greek word translated "communicated" in verse 15 denotes not casual charity but covenant cooperation—a fellowship of joint participation that set the Philippian church apart as Paul's singular missions partner.

context: verses 16 through 18. Paul writes, For even in Thessalonica ye sent once again unto my necessity, not because I desire a gift, but I desire fruit that may abound to your account. But I have all and abound. I am full, having received of Epaphroditus the things which were sent from you, an odor of a sweet smell, a sacrifice acceptable, well-pleasing to God.
point_summary: Philippians 4:16 illustrates that the Philippians' repeated giving to Paul, even amid their own limitations, exemplified sacrificial participation rather than silent passivity.

context: Paul commends them in verse 16 for sending once and again, once and again unto his necessity
point_summary: Philippians 4:16's phrase "once and again" marks the Philippians' giving as persistent, repeated sacrifice offered not from surplus but from deep poverty, commended precisely for its consistency rather than its amount.

context: verse 16 teaches, they sent once and again unto whose necessity? Unto Paul's necessity
point_summary: Persistent, repeated financial support—sending "once and again unto his necessity"—is commended as the hallmark of the Philippian church's giving and held as a model for ongoing missionary support.

context: fruit would abound to whose account? To their account, to the church's account
point_summary: Giving that is purposeful, persistent, and proven yields fruit credited to the giver's own account, demonstrating that God keeps careful record of sacrificial missionary support.

context: But I have all, and abound. I am full, having received of Epaphroditus the things which were sent from you, an odor of a sweet smell, a sacrifice acceptable, well-pleasing to God
point_summary: The Philippian church's monetary gift is described in sacrificial, priestly language — an odor of a sweet smell, acceptable and well-pleasing to God — moving it out of the economic realm into the realm of worship.

context: what Paul is doing in verse 18 was assuring his friends in the Macedonian colony of Philippi that their gift might have reached him on the earth

context: in Philippians chapter 4, God, through the pen of the apostle Paul, makes it abundantly clear that missions giving isn't just horizontal

context: it becomes a sweet-smelling savor in the nostrils of God
